name: agentic-structure
description: Collaborative programming framework for production-ready development. Use when starting features, writing code, handling security/errors, adding comments, discussing requirements, or encountering knowledge gaps. Applies to all development tasks for clear, safe, maintainable code.
capabilities: Guides implementation order from core logic to interface
Provides security-first coding guidelines and secrets handling
Defines error handling patterns and recovery strategies
Establishes discussion protocols for ambiguous requirements
Documents commenting and knowledge sharing workflows
Enforces coding standards and file organization rules
author: BackGwa
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidelines. No executable code, no network operations, no command execution. The skill specifies allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob only. All 142 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES caused by the scanner detecting security terminology in documentation (e.g., 'Do not use MD5' flagged as 'weak crypto'). The skill-report.json audit correctly identified this as safe.
valueStatement: Developers struggle with inconsistent code quality and security gaps when working with AI assistants. This skill provides comprehensive guidelines for collaborative development including coding standards, error handling, security practices, and decision-making protocols.
limitations: Does not execute code or modify files directly
Does not integrate with external APIs or services
Does not provide runtime validation or testing
Requires user to apply guidelines manually in their workflow

useCases.title: Establish team coding standards
useCases.description: Set consistent coding conventions, error handling patterns, and security practices across all AI-assisted development work.

useCases.title: Enforce security-first development
useCases.description: Apply security guidelines for secrets management, authentication, rate limiting, and input validation in all code generated.

useCases.title: Guide collaborative decision-making
useCases.description: Define when AI assistants should ask for clarification versus proceeding autonomously based on architectural impact.

promptTemplates.title: Start New Feature
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to implement [feature description]. Apply the Development Process guidelines to help me plan this feature. Start by identifying the core logic, integration points, and interface components I need to build.

promptTemplates.title: Handle Ambiguity
promptTemplates.prompt: My request is unclear: [original request]. Use the Discussion Guidelines to identify the ambiguous terms and ask me clarifying questions to determine the single correct interpretation.

promptTemplates.title: Write Secure Code
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to [task description]. Apply the Security Guidelines to ensure this implementation uses proper secrets handling, authentication, authorization, and abuse prevention. Report any security concerns before proceeding.

promptTemplates.title: Refactor Complex Code
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this code section: [code or description]. Apply the Coding Standards to identify nesting issues, abstraction opportunities, and refactoring triggers. Explain what changes would improve maintainability.

bestPractices: Apply security guidelines first when handling sensitive data or user input - security always takes precedence over other considerations.
Use the guideline matrix to select the right reference for each task rather than reading all documentation upfront.
When requirements are ambiguous, pause and ask clarifying questions rather than proceeding with assumptions.
antiPatterns: Proceeding with implementation when multiple valid approaches exist without user alignment on trade-offs.
Adding comments that merely restate what the code does rather than explaining why the code exists.
Using try-catch blocks broadly without specific recovery strategies or meaningful error context.

faq.question: Which AI tools support this skill?
faq.answer: This skill works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. Apply guidelines when writing code, handling errors, or making architectural decisions.

faq.question: How many guidelines should I read at once?
faq.answer: Use the Guideline Selection Matrix in SKILL.md to find the relevant document for your current task. Read only what you need.

faq.question: Do I need to follow all guidelines simultaneously?
faq.answer: No. When guidelines conflict, follow the priority order: Security first, then Development Process, then Error Handling, Coding Standards, and Comments last.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This is a prompt-only skill with no code execution, network access, or file operations. It only provides development guidance.

faq.question: What if I disagree with a guideline?
faq.answer: Apply judgment based on context. The guidelines state that progress over perfection is acceptable - moving in the right direction matters more than perfect adherence.

faq.question: How does this compare to writing code directly?
faq.answer: This skill does not write code for you. It provides prompts and frameworks that help AI assistants produce higher-quality, more secure, and more maintainable code.
